summary: Furio Dondero is one of my mining partners and a very colorful Nevadan born individual. Even though he's getting elderly, he's still alive and we've always been associated in mining together. He holds a quarter interest in the five mining claims we have in the Ruby Hills of Eureka County. Our partnership also includes retired General Dale Smith and Hugh Shamberger, both former state engineers.

Retired General Dale Smith is one of the four partners holding our mining claims in Nevada. His father was 'Long Tom' Smith, a wonderful man I knew when he was the state engineer. Dale's involvement in our mining ventures has been significant, and he shares a quarter interest in the claims located in the Ruby Hills of Eureka County.

Hugh Shamberger was the state engineer of Nevada for many years and is one of my partners in holding mining claims. Along with Furio Dondero and Dale Smith, we share interests in patented claims in the Ruby Hills mining district of Eureka County. His experience as a state engineer has been valuable in our mining endeavors.

The Ruby Hills in Eureka County is where our mining claims are located. We originally held thirteen claims called the 'Lucky Thirteen,' but sold eight to a Canadian company that tried to develop them. The area is rich in lead, zinc, and some silver, but water and pumping costs have made mining economically challenging.

Eureka County, Nevada, is where the Ruby Hills mining district is located, and where I hold mining claims with my partners. The mining history there is notable, though economic challenges like water pumping have hindered development. I hope the county will see another prosperous mining era in the future.

A Canadian company once bought eight of our thirteen mining claims in the Ruby Hills, Eureka County. They explored and attempted to develop the claims but eventually abandoned the project due to the high cost of pumping water. This experience showed the difficulties foreign companies can face in Nevada mining.

Lovelock, Nevada, is where my father ran a grocery store located in what is now the Turrillas building. We lived there until about 1918 when a fire destroyed our home in the Fairview district nearby. After that, my family moved to Fallon, but Lovelock was an important place in my early life and schooling.

Fairview, Nevada, was where our ranch was located in the upper Lovelock Valley. I attended a two-room, eight-grade school there, riding a horse about a mile to get to class. Our home in Fairview was destroyed by a fire when I was about nine years old, which led to our family's move to Fallon.

We moved to Fallon, Nevada, around 1918 or 1919 after our home in Fairview burned down. My father bought a grocery store called Peoples Brothers there, which was run by early farmers from the Fernley and Fallon districts. I finished grammar school and high school in Fallon and later attended the University of Nevada from there.

I remember vividly one evening when my family and I were returning home from a movie in Lovelock and saw the northern skies beautifully lit up. We thought it was the aurora borealis, which can sometimes be seen as far south as Nevada. However, as we got closer to our home, we realized the light was from a fire that had destroyed our house in Fairview.

Peoples Brothers was a grocery store in Fallon, Nevada, run by early farmers from the Fernley and Fallon districts. After our home burned down, my father seized the opportunity to buy this store, which was the main reason for our family's move to Fallon around 1918 or 1919. It allowed him to continue his work as a grocer.

I attended the University of Nevada, Reno after finishing high school in Fallon. My mother also attended the university for two years before becoming a schoolteacher. Later in life, I returned to the university as a student, where I was humorously nicknamed 'Old Testament' because of my last name Bible.

My mother, Isabel Welch, was born in Unionville, Nevada, and was the only girl among six or seven siblings. She was the family historian and a schoolteacher when my father married her in 1909. Her father, Robert Welch, was born in Brechin, Scotland, and she had Scotch-Irish ancestry. Sadly, much of her family history was lost in the fire that destroyed our home.

Unionville, Nevada, is where my mother, Isabel Welch, was born and where my aunt Ethel Welch was also born. It was part of my mother's early life before she moved to Lovelock. The town holds significance in my family genealogy and history.

Brechin is a place in Scotland where my maternal grandfather, Robert Welch, was born. My mother told me he was a professor at the University of Edinburgh, though I was unable to verify this during a visit to Edinburgh. Brechin connects my family to our Scottish roots.

Scotland is where my mother's family originated, specifically from Brechin. My grandfather, Robert Welch, was born there and was said to have been a professor at the University of Edinburgh. My wife and I visited Edinburgh years ago to trace this heritage, though I couldn't confirm all the details.

My maternal grandfather, Robert Welch, was said by my mother to have been a professor at the University of Edinburgh in Scotland. I tried to verify this during a visit to Edinburgh but was unable to find records confirming it. Nonetheless, this university is part of my family's lore and Scottish connection.

Winnemucca, Nevada, is where my aunt Ethel Welch lived her entire life. She was married to my uncle Will Welch, my mother's brother. Winnemucca is part of my family's extended history and genealogy in Nevada.

Iowa is part of my family's broader geographic background, specifically Estherville, Iowa, which is mentioned in relation to genealogical research. While my immediate family lived in Nevada, Iowa represents part of the wider context of our ancestry and migration.

Estherville, Iowa, is noted in my genealogical research as part of the family's extended history. Although my family primarily lived in Nevada, places like Estherville help trace our broader roots. It's a location connected to the study of our ancestry.
Agnes Mcllrey is not directly mentioned in my family story or genealogy as far as I recall. Therefore, I do not have a personal connection or story involving her in my history.
Auckland, New Zealand, is not part of my personal or family history as I know it. I have no direct connection or experience related to Auckland in my life story.
New Zealand does not figure into my family history or personal experiences. I have no known ties or stories connected to New Zealand in my genealogy or life events.
Australia is not part of my family's background or my personal history. I have no stories or connections related to Australia in the context of my life or genealogy.

Robert Welch was my maternal grandfather, born in Brechin, Scotland. My mother told me he was a professor at the University of Edinburgh, though I could not confirm this. He later had a small ranch outside Lovelock, Nevada, where my mother grew up and attended school.
James Welch is not mentioned in my family history or oral account, so I do not have any personal stories or connections related to him.
Alexander Welch does not appear in my family narrative or genealogy as I know it, and I have no personal relationship or story involving him.
Coldstream is not referenced in my family history or personal story, so I have no connection or memories related to this place.
Jane Welch is not mentioned in my family history or oral account, so I do not have any personal stories or connections related to her.
George Murray is not part of my family history or personal narrative as I know it, and I have no stories or connections involving him.
The British Museum is not mentioned in my family history or personal experiences, so I have no direct connection or story involving it.
The London Gazette does not appear in my family history or personal story, and I have no association or recollection regarding it.
World War I is not directly mentioned in my personal or family history during this oral account. Therefore, I do not have specific stories or connections to share about it.
Company B-1 is not referenced in my family history or personal narrative, so I have no connection or stories related to this military unit.
